hypothesis: When an agent's iterative discovery history within MADE is used as a structured context (encoding the sequence of proposed compositions, their energies above the hull, and resulting convex hull updates), a large language model fine-tuned on this sequential feedback will develop an implicit surrogate model of the chemical landscape that enables it to outperform both random and diversity-based acquisition functions in sample efficiency—measured as novel stable compounds discovered per oracle query—specifically because the autoregressive reasoning over ordered failure-and-success trajectories encodes transferable chemical heuristics (e.g., valence balance, electronegativity matching) that generalize across chemical systems unseen during fine-tuning, a prediction that is falsifiable by comparing per-query discovery rates across held-out ternary and quaternary systems against non-sequential baselines that receive the same information in randomized order.
